method3.generated_article:
==Etymology==
Tromelin Island was formerly known as the Isle of Sand.[1] Its present name honours Jacques Marie Boudin de Tromelin de La Nuguy, captain of the French corvette Dauphine, who reached the island on 29 November 1776 and rescued eight enslaved Malagasy survivors who had been stranded there for 15 years.[1]

==Description==
Tromelin Island is a small, low-lying coral island in the Indian Ocean, situated about 450 kilometres east of Madagascar and 500 kilometres north of Réunion.[1] It is approximately 1 square kilometre in area and has been described as almond- or sunflower-seed-shaped, measuring about 1,700 metres at its longest point and 700 metres at its widest.[1][2][4] The island is extremely flat, with its highest point only about 7 metres above sea level and a coastline of less than 4 kilometres.[4] Its surface consists mainly of sand, coral and sparse scrub vegetation, with little shade and plants kept low by wind exposure.[2][4]

The island is uninhabited except for a small staffed scientific and meteorological station, and it also has an unpaved runway and a few associated buildings, cisterns and concrete structures.[1][2][5] France administers Tromelin Island as part of the Scattered Islands district of the French Southern and Antarctic Lands.[1] Its meteorological station supports regional weather forecasting and cyclone monitoring, reflecting the island’s position in the cyclone corridor of the south-west Indian Ocean.[1][2][4] From late October to May, cyclones can inundate low parts of the island, damage turtle and bird nesting sites, and severely affect wildlife reproduction.[4]

Tromelin is an important nesting site for sea turtles and seabirds, and it is also home to large numbers of hermit crabs.[1][2][4] The accidental introduction of rats caused major ecological damage, reducing native bird diversity and leaving only small populations of masked and red-footed boobies.[3][5] French scientists and authorities eradicated the rodents in 2005 using poisoned bait, after which native vegetation began to recover and several bird species returned.[3][5] The island is now regarded as a valuable reference ecosystem for environmental research and a notable example of island restoration following invasive mammal eradication.[3][5]

==Fauna and flora==
Tromelin’s native land vertebrate fauna is limited, with no native terrestrial mammals, reptiles or amphibians; green sea turtles (*Chelonia mydas*) are the main non-avian vertebrates using the island for breeding.[1] Seabirds dominate the fauna, and historical evidence indicates that six to eight species bred there before predation by introduced Norway rats (*Rattus norvegicus*) greatly reduced the community.[1][2] By 2005, only two booby species still bred on the island, and their reproductive success was limited.[1] Following the eradication of rats in 2005, the breeding seabird assemblage recovered naturally, increasing to seven breeding species and from 353 to 4,758 breeding pairs by 2022.[1] The masked booby (*Sula dactylatra melanops*), described as an endemic subspecies, is among the principal breeding seabirds, and red-footed boobies also nest on the island.[1][2] Between 2017 and 2021, eleven non-breeding seabird species were recorded, including roseate, common, Saunders’s and lesser crested terns, brown skua, brown booby, Mascarene petrel, great and lesser frigatebirds, and both white-tailed and red-tailed tropicbirds.[3] Great and lesser frigatebirds used the island regularly as a roost, while the two tropicbird species occasionally landed or displayed, suggesting possible future colonisation.[3] Mice are now the only introduced mammals reported as present on the island.[3]

The flora is a sparse littoral plant community adapted to wind, salt spray and limited freshwater.[1] Before major invasive-species impacts, the native vegetation was dominated by tree heliotrope, *Tournefortia argentea* (syn. *Heliotropium foertherianum*), together with fewer than ten herbaceous species and no native trees.[1] Rat predation on seeds, seedlings and foliage severely reduced native plant cover before eradication measures in 2005–2006.[1] Subsequent monitoring recorded a recovery of native vegetation, with seven native plant taxa present by 2013 and increased density and height of *Tournefortia argentea*.[1] Introduced plants, including a small number of coconut palms (*Cocos nucifera*), persist but do not dominate the vegetation.[1] The recovery of vegetation has been identified as one of the ecological effects of rat eradication, alongside the expansion of seabird populations and increased nutrient transfer through guano.[5]

==History==
In the 1720s, the French navigator Jean Marie Briand de la Feuillée recorded the island and named it Île de Sable, or “Isle of Sand”.[1] Its later name commemorates Captain Chevalier de Tromelin, whose ship, the Dauphine, reached the island on 29 November 1776.[1][3]

In July 1761, the French ship Utile wrecked near Tromelin while carrying an illicit cargo of enslaved people.[4] Twenty sailors and at least seventy enslaved people died, with many of the latter trapped below deck after the hatches had been locked or nailed down.[4] The surviving castaways remained on the island for fifteen years, until Tromelin rescued seven women and an eight-month-old child in 1776 and took them to Mauritius, where they were later freed.[3][4] British sailors later reported seeing remnants of “stone” houses and neatly arranged graves on the island.[4]

A later UNESCO-sponsored archaeological investigation by French archaeologists and divers examined the wreck and excavated parts of the island.[4] The team found walls of dwellings built from coral blocks and compacted sand, as well as a large oven containing remains of turtles, birds and shellfish.[4] These finds have been interpreted as evidence of the castaways’ organization and resilience, as well as of the brutality of their abandonment.[4] The episode has since been treated in documentaries, archaeological reports and journalism as a historical symbol of slavery, isolation and survival.[2]

France bases its sovereignty claim on the arrival of the Dauphine on 29 November 1776.[1] Mauritius bases its claim on the argument that Tromelin should have been ceded to the United Kingdom under the Treaty of Paris of 1814 and therefore should not have continued to be administered by France as a dependency of Réunion.[1] The United Nations has not recognized Mauritian sovereignty over Tromelin.[1] In 1954, France constructed a meteorological station and a landing strip on the island.[1]

==Climate==
Tromelin Island has a tropical savanna climate under the Köppen classification, with consistently warm conditions and a marked wet season from November to April associated with the intertropical convergence zone.[1]  
The mean annual temperature is about 26.7 °C, with little seasonal variation because of the island’s tropical oceanic setting.[1]  
Annual precipitation is approximately 1,074 mm, falling mainly during the warmer months; February is among the wettest months, while May to October is comparatively dry.[1]  
Prevailing southeast trade winds are common and can be strong, contributing to high evaporation and exposed maritime conditions on the low-lying coral island.[1]  
Tromelin’s position in the western Indian Ocean makes it useful for cyclone monitoring, and the island has long hosted a weather station used for regional forecasting.[3]
